title: Networks, the Internet & E-commerce
simple_html:
A network is just two or more computers connected so they can share data and resources. The Internet is the world's largest network — a network of networks — that lets any connected device talk to any other using common rules called protocols (the main one is TCP/IP). E-commerce is buying and selling goods, services, or information over these networks.

Why it matters

Networks let a business reach customers anywhere, coordinate suppliers in real time, and let employees work from anywhere. The Internet collapsed distance and cost: a small shop can now sell globally for almost nothing. E-commerce turned that reach into revenue and reshaped whole industries (think retail, travel, and media).

How it works (in plain terms)

Data is broken into small packets, each labeled with an address, sent across the network, and reassembled at the other end. Every device has an IP address (its number); friendly names like a website address are translated to that number by the DNS (Domain Name System). The Web is one service that runs on the Internet, using browsers and links.

Key takeaways

  • The Internet is a network of networks; the Web is just one application on top of it (email and streaming are others).
  • Protocols (TCP/IP) are the shared rules that make global connection possible.
  • E-commerce models are often labeled by who trades with whom: B2C (business to consumer), B2B (business to business), and C2C (consumer to consumer).
  • Networks create network effects: each new user makes the network more valuable to everyone.
  • M-commerce (selling via phones) and the Internet of Things (everyday objects online) are the fast-growing frontier.

Bottom line: networks are the plumbing of the digital economy, and e-commerce is what flows through them to create value.
intermediate_html:
This lesson connects the technical foundation (networks and the Internet) to the business activity it enables (e-commerce). You should be able to explain how data moves, name the major e-commerce models, and describe why digital markets behave differently from physical ones.

Network and Internet fundamentals

A network links devices to share data and resources. Networks are classified by reach: a LAN (Local Area Network) covers a building, a WAN (Wide Area Network) spans cities or countries, and the Internet interconnects them all. The Internet works because every device speaks the same suite of protocols, TCP/IP. TCP breaks messages into packets and guarantees they arrive complete and in order; IP handles addressing and routing each packet toward its destination. Human-friendly domain names are resolved to numeric IP addresses by DNS. The World Wide Web is one service layered on the Internet, delivered via HTTP/HTTPS and viewed in browsers; email, VoIP, and streaming are separate services using the same plumbing.

From connectivity to commerce

E-commerce is the use of the Internet and Web to transact business. It is typically organized by the parties involved:

ModelWho tradesExample
B2CBusiness to consumerOnline retailer selling to shoppers
B2BBusiness to businessWholesale marketplace, e-procurement
C2CConsumer to consumerAuction and resale platforms
M-commerceAny of the above via mobileApp-based ordering and payments

Why digital markets are different

Three properties matter for the exam. First, information density and transparency fall in cost while rising in quality, so prices and products are easier to compare. Second, disintermediation can occur: by selling directly online, producers remove middlemen and capture their margin, though new re-intermediaries (aggregators, platforms) often emerge. Third, the Long Tail: near-zero shelf and distribution cost lets sellers profit from many low-demand niche items, not just a few hits.

Network effects and platforms

Many digital businesses are two-sided platforms connecting two groups (e.g., buyers and sellers, riders and drivers). Their value rises with participation — a network effect — which can create winner-take-most markets and strong switching barriers once a platform leads.

Worked example: choosing a channel

A regional coffee roaster sells wholesale to cafes (B2B) and wants to add direct online sales. Going B2C via its own site means disintermediating distributors: it keeps the distributor's margin but must now handle marketing, payments, and shipping. Listing instead on a large marketplace gives instant reach and trust (leveraging the platform's network effect) but pays a commission and surrenders the customer relationship. The decision hinges on margin math versus reach, and on who owns the customer data — a recurring strategic trade-off.

How to apply it

  • Map any business to a model (B2C/B2B/C2C) and identify whether it is a pipeline or a platform.
  • Ask whether the Internet enables disintermediation here, and who might re-intermediate.
  • Look for Long Tail opportunities where digital distribution makes niches profitable.
  • Assess network effects: does each new user increase value, and does that create a moat?
full_html:
Networks and the Internet are the infrastructure of the digital economy; e-commerce is the commercial activity that infrastructure makes possible. Mastery means understanding the technical stack well enough to reason about business consequences — cost structures, market microstructure, strategy, and risk. This lesson builds from packets to platforms and links to adjacent topics on competitive advantage and security.

The technical foundation: how the Internet actually works

A network is a set of connected devices sharing data and resources, classified by geographic reach as LAN, MAN, or WAN. Internetworking succeeds because of a layered reference model. The conceptual OSI seven-layer model and the practical four-layer TCP/IP model both separate concerns: physical transmission, link, network (routing), transport (reliable delivery), and application. Packet switching — dividing messages into independently routed packets — was the breakthrough that made the Internet resilient and efficient versus the dedicated circuits of the telephone era (Baran, 1964; Leiner et al., 2009). TCP ensures ordered, error-checked delivery; IP provides global addressing. The Domain Name System resolves names to addresses, and the World Wide Web (Berners-Lee, 1989) is an application layered atop the Internet using HTTP/HTTPS, URLs, and hypertext. Crucially, the Web is not the Internet; email, VoIP, IoT telemetry, and streaming are co-equal services. (See the related lesson on Telecommunications and Internet Architecture for the deeper stack.)

Economic properties of digital markets

Laudon and Laudon (2022) identify features that distinguish online from physical markets: ubiquity (marketspace replaces marketplace), global reach, universal standards (one technical standard worldwide lowers entry cost), information richness and density, interactivity, personalization/customization, and social technology. Together these collapse search costs and transaction costs. Lower transaction costs were predicted to shrink firm boundaries (Coase, 1937; Malone, Yates & Benjamin, 1987): when coordinating through the market is cheap, firms outsource more and markets expand relative to hierarchies.

Business models and classification

E-commerce is classified by participants — B2C, B2B (the largest by value), C2C, and increasingly B2B2C — and by revenue model: advertising, sales, subscription, freemium, transaction fee, and affiliate. M-commerce extends all of these to mobile, adding location awareness. Anderson's (2006) Long Tail shows that near-zero inventory and distribution costs let firms monetize vast catalogs of niche demand that physical shelves could never carry; aggregate niche sales can rival hit sales.

Disintermediation, reintermediation, and value chains

The Internet lets producers reach customers directly, disintermediating wholesalers and retailers and capturing their margin while shortening the value chain. Yet pure disintermediation is often overstated: new intermediaries — search engines, marketplaces, comparison and review sites — emerge to reduce search costs, a process of reintermediation (Sarkar, Butler & Steinfield, 1995). The strategic question is not whether intermediaries vanish but which intermediary controls the customer relationship and the data.

Platforms and network effects

Many of the most valuable digital firms are two-sided (or multi-sided) platforms that mediate exchange between distinct user groups (Rochet & Tirole, 2003; Parker, Van Alstyne & Choudary, 2016). Their economics are governed by direct and cross-side network effects: more buyers attract more sellers and vice versa. Metcalfe's Law heuristically states a network's value scales with the square of its users. Strong network effects produce increasing returns, tipping, and winner-take-most outcomes (Shapiro & Varian, 1999), raising switching costs and creating durable competitive advantage — directly relevant to the lesson on IS and Competitive Advantage and Porter's Competitive Forces Model.

Worked example: build-your-own versus platform listing

Suppose a brand earns $40 gross margin per unit selling wholesale. Direct B2C would add roughly $25/unit in marketing and fulfillment but capture an extra $20 of channel margin, netting near-break-even initially while building a first-party data asset. Listing on a dominant marketplace yields instant reach (cross-side network effects) but a 15% commission on an $80 price ($12/unit) and, critically, no ownership of the customer relationship. The numerically close decision tilts on intangibles: data ownership, brand control, and long-run pricing power versus the platform. This mirrors real channel-conflict dilemmas and shows why complementary assets (fulfillment, brand, data) determine who wins online.

Nuances and debates

Three debates recur. (1) Net neutrality: whether carriers may prioritize traffic shapes who can compete online. (2) Platform power and antitrust: network effects that benefit users can also entrench monopolies and enable self-preferencing. (3) Privacy versus personalization: the data that powers personalization raises governance and regulatory exposure (links to the lessons on Security & Control and IS Ethics).

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Equating the Web with the Internet — the Web is one application among many.
  • Assuming the Internet always removes intermediaries; reintermediation is common.
  • Treating B2C as the biggest market — B2B is far larger by transaction value.
  • Confusing a marketplace platform (multi-sided, network effects) with a simple online store (pipeline).
  • Ignoring that lower transaction costs reshape make-vs-buy and firm boundaries, not just retail.

Cross-links

This lesson underpins IS & Competitive Advantage (platforms and network effects as moats), connects forward to Security & Control (every networked channel widens the attack surface), and to Building Systems & IS Ethics (privacy of the data e-commerce generates). Foundationally it rests on the network architecture introduced in earlier infrastructure lessons.
